title: The Resurgence Of The “New Three”
text:
The Resurgence Of The “New Three”

Wei He

Gavekal Dragonomics | 20 Jul 2026

The “new three” are back: though no longer so new, they are still the most high-profile examples of the trade tensions generated by China’s manufacturing prowess. In 2023, China’s government applied that label to three fast-growing products that symbolized the success of industrial policy and the country’s move up the value chain: electric vehicles, lithium-ion batteries and solar cells. Exports of these products rose so rapidly that China’s trading partners alleged they were pushed onto global markets by domestic excess capacity, rather than being pulled by global demand for high-quality goods.On a trip to China in 2024, then-US Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen explicitly referred to the “new three” to warn that China’s overcapacity was “simply too large for the rest of the world to absorb.” China’s state media fired back that “equating more export products to excess capacity is completely unfounded,” and instead attributed the rapid export gains to rising global demand and “the efficiency of Chinese enterprises.” Yet at the time of Yellen’s comments, exports of the new three had already plateaued after their 2021-22 surge.
